coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel monomeric graphene quantum dots and corresponding nanocomposite with molecularly imprinted polymer for electrochemical detection of an anticancerous ifosfamide drug
coredata.dc:creator: Bali Prasad, Bhim
Kumar, Anil
Singh, Ragini
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports a typical synthesis of a nanocomposite of functionalized graphene quantum dots and imprinted polymer at the surface of screen-printed carbon electrode using N-acryloyl-4-aminobenzamide, as a functional monomer, and an anticancerous drug, ifosfamide, as a print molecule (test analyte). Herein, graphene quantum dots in nanocomposite practically induced the electrocatalytic activity by lowering the oxidation overpotential of test analyte and thereby amplifying electronic transmission, without any interfacial barrier in between the film and the electrode surface. The differential pulse anodic stripping signal at functionalized graphene quantum dots based imprinted sensor was realized to be about 3- and 7-fold higher as compared to the traditionally made imprinted polymers prepared in the presence and the absence of graphene quantum dots (un-functionalized), respectively. This may be attributed to a pertinent synergism in between the positively charged functionalized graphene quantum dots in the film and the target analyte toward the enhancement of electro-conductivity of the film and thereby the electrode kinetics. In fact, the covalent attachment of graphene quantum dots with N-acryloyl-4-aminobenzamide molecules might exert an extended conjugation at their interface facilitating electro conducting to render the channelized pathways for the electron transport. The proposed sensor is practically applicable to the ultratrace evaluation of ifosfamide in real (biological/pharmaceutical) samples with detection limit as low as 0.11ngmL−1 (S/N=3), without any matrix effect, cross-reactivity, and false-positives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Monomeric graphene quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Electrochemical sensor
Ifosfamide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of the modified SPCEs
Spectral, morphological, surface area and conducting characterizations
Analysis in aqueous and real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XPS spectra of GQDs and m-GQDs. C1s high resolution XPS spectra of GQDs (B) and m-GQDs (C).

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of (a) bare- SPCE, (b) GQDs, (c) m-GQDs, (d) GQDs -MIP, and (e) m-GQDs -MIP modified SPCEs [Phosphate buffer solution (pH =5.0) containing 0.1mM [Fe (CN)6]3-/4-, ν=100mVs−1 and (B) DPASV runs of IFO (50ngmL−1): (a) bare-SPCE, (b) GQDs, (c) m-GQDs, (d) MIP-film, (e) GQDs -MIP, and (f) m-GQDs -MIP modified SPCEs.

(A) CV runs on m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE (IFO concentration 14.77ngmL−1) at different scan rates: (a) 10, (b) 50, (c) 100, and (d) 200mVs−1. (B) DPASV responses of different IFO concentrations (from a-h): 0.00 (blank), 0.25, 3.40, 13.72, 45.78, 70.93, 91.58, and 121.35ngmL−1 at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE [Inset: Calibration plots for m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE (R
2 =0.99) and m-GQDs -NIP@SPCE (R
2 =0.88), showing saturation at 121.35 and 78.23ngmL−1, respectively. (C) DPASV response on m-GQDs -NIP@SPCE for IFO (27.0ngmL−1) in blood plasma (curve a), urine (curve e), and pharmaceutical sample (curve i) and on m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE in blood plasma (curves b, c, d), urine (curves f, g, h), and pharmaceutical (curves j, k, l) samples spiked with 10.90, 49.13, and 95.61ngmL−1 IFO, respectively.

SEM images: (A) m-GQDs -MIP adduct, (B) m-GQDs -MIP, and (C) side view of m-GQDs -MIP modification at SPCEs. TEM images: (D) GQDs and (E) m-GQDs. (F) EDS investigation of m-GQDs -MIP immobilized on SPCE.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of m-GQDs and fabrication of m-GQDs -MIP modified SPCE.
Analytical results for DPASV measurements in aqueous, blood plasma, urine and pharmaceutical samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % Recovery =(amount of analyte determined/amount of analyte taken)×100.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: LOD based on the minimum distinguishable signal for lower concentrations of analyte (S/N =3, 95% confidence level).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD (%) for three sets of LOD data.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: diluted 11.5×107 times, endogenous concentration is 37×107 μgmL−1, certified values 40×103 μgmL−1.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel monomeric graphene quantum dots and corresponding nanocomposite with molecularly imprinted polymer for electrochemical detection of an anticancerous ifosfamide drug

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Division, Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analytical Division, Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports a typical synthesis of a nanocomposite of functionalized graphene quantum dots and imprinted polymer at the surface of screen-printed carbon electrode using N-acryloyl-4-aminobenzamide, as a functional monomer, and an anticancerous drug, ifosfamide, as a print molecule (test analyte). Herein, graphene quantum dots in nanocomposite practically induced the electrocatalytic activity by lowering the oxidation overpotential of test analyte and thereby amplifying electronic transmission, without any interfacial barrier in between the film and the electrode surface. The differential pulse anodic stripping signal at functionalized graphene quantum dots based imprinted sensor was realized to be about 3- and 7-fold higher as compared to the traditionally made imprinted polymers prepared in the presence and the absence of graphene quantum dots (un-functionalized), respectively. This may be attributed to a pertinent synergism in between the positively charged functionalized graphene quantum dots in the film and the target analyte toward the enhancement of electro-conductivity of the film and thereby the electrode kinetics. In fact, the covalent attachment of graphene quantum dots with N-acryloyl-4-aminobenzamide molecules might exert an extended conjugation at their interface facilitating electro conducting to render the channelized pathways for the electron transport. The proposed sensor is practically applicable to the ultratrace evaluation of ifosfamide in real (biological/pharmaceutical) samples with detection limit as low as 0.11ngmL−1 (S/N=3), without any matrix effect, cross-reactivity, and false-positives.

ce:section.ce:para: Ifosfamide, N, 3-Bis(2-chloroethyl)-1,3,2-oxazaphosphinan-2-amide 2-oxide (IFO), is used for the treatment of sarcomas, lymphoma, gynecologic and testicular cancers as an anticancerous drug (Kilickap et al., 2006). With the widespread use of IFO in oncology, its toxic effect, however, may pose a serious issue in the treatment of cancer patients. IFO and its metabolites can cross the blood brain barrier causing central nervous system toxicities as indicated by metabolic encephalopathy of varying severity, confusion, lethargy, agitation, somnolence, and seizure. The high therapeutic doses of IFO are associated with severe nephrotoxicity in the form of Fanconi syndrome (Rossi et al., 1994; Skinner et al., 1993). Thus, the determination of IFO level in real samples (blood plasma, urine, and pharmaceutics) is an important analytical agenda so as to decide the adequate doses for chemotherapy.

Many chromatographic methods viz., high performance liquid chromatography- mass spectrometry (Storme et al., 2005; Sottani et al., 2008; Deroussent et al., 2015) and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Lambrechts et al., 1991; Sannolo et al., 1999) have been reported for the detection of IFO. However, these methods require elaborate pre-treatment and are practically cumbersome. On the other hand, electroanalytical techniques could be made simple, fast, and automated easily for designing a portable device. However, the major obstacles are high overpotential of IFO oxidation and severe interferences from the co-existing materials, which might hamper the selectivity and sensitivity of the measurement (Paci et al., 2001; Baj-Rossi et al., 2012). In order to overcome these attendant problems, selective molecular recognition systems like molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), can be utilized. MIP is synthesized simply by the polymerization of a functional monomer in the presence of a template molecule, and the specific cavities with complementary size, shape, and orientation of functionalities are left after the template retrieval. These ‘‘molecularly designed cavities’’ show a perfect affinity for the template molecule over other structurally related compounds. Accordingly, the anti-interference ability of molecular recognition element in complex matrices can be expected (Afkhami et al., 2013; Piletsky and Turner, 2002).

Detection sensitivity of an imprinted sensor is not only based on the amount of effective imprinted sites on the surface of the sensor, but also governed by the use of nanomaterials (such as carbon based nanomaterials, metal nanoparticles and metal quantum dots) in the coated film at the electrode surface (Luo et al., 2015). Traditional metal quantum dots contain heavy metals, such as Cd, and their applications are thus limited due to the toxicity and potential environmental risk of the heavy metals. Therefore, the development of eco-friendly alternatives is highly desirable. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a metal-free system, show strong potential for replacing traditional metal QDs. Graphene often regarded as the new “miracle material” that attracted increasing attentions due to large surface area, fast electron transfer rate, and excellent electrical conductivity (Novoselov et al., 2012; Chen et al., 2012). Nevertheless, strong hydrophobic nature of graphene may be deterrent for the analyte recapture in aqueous solutions, presumably due to the interfacial resistance involved. On the other hand, GQDs are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm with a unique sp2 and sp3 hybrid structure (Dong et al., 2012; Jiang et al., 2013). GQDs are reckoned as an alternative material with good conductivity and high surface area due to their low dimensions (Benitez-Martinez and Valcarcel, 2015). GQDs can separate the charges generated at the interface and provide charge transporting pathway to the electrode. Furthermore, the carboxylic moieties at GQDs edges help water dispersibility where their functional groups could easily be complexed with several compounds (including in/organic, polymeric, or biological compounds) (Shen et al., 2012). On conversion of graphene sheets (2D) into GQDs (0D), GQDs might exhibit a new orientation for the complexation due to quantum confinement and edge effects (Li et al., 2013). The abundance of hydrophilic edges as well as the hydrophobic plane in GQDs reportedly enhances the analyte absorption on the electrode surface (Sun et al., 2013). Furthermore, it can act as multivalent redox species representing an exciting opportunity for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors (Shinde and Pillai, 2013). It might be noted that a novel composite of GQDs and MIP for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol was prepared but terminal C˭C bond was missing which could anchor the polymer without any crippling (Zhou et al., 2014). The covalent functionalization of GQDs with a monomer greatly improved solubility and restricted the acute problem of film crippling as encountered with the pristine GQDs and C60 immobilizations (Tan et al., 2016; Carano et al., 2002). Notably, such functionalized monomeric GQDs (m-GQDs) with terminal C˭C double bonds are not yet explored for MIP synthesis.

Various research efforts have been put to obtain biosensors (Zhao et al., 2011; Roushani and Abdi, 2014; Razmi and Mohammad-Rezaei, 2013) and MIP-based sensors (Tan et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2015; Zor et al., 2015) using pristine GQDs. However, these sensors also suffered from the acute problem of falling off of GQDs from the electrode surface during the measurement. Fortunately this problem was resolved if one used a stabilized form of GQDs. In this work, the stable m-GQDs were obtained by covalent binding of GQDs using monomeric units of N-Acryloyl-4-aminobenzamide (N-ABA), through amide linkages. Herein, the built-in-potential at the interface of N-ABA and GQDs may increase the charge separation which can accelerate the charge transport to electrode through high mobility media of GQDs, in the same manner as has been reported earlier for poly(3-hexylthiophene) - GQDs composite (Kong, 2015). Keeping abreast with this advantage, the m-GQDs -MIP based nanocomposite modified screen printed carbon electrode (m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE) is fabricated for the first time. The proposed sensor showed an excellent electrocatalytic activity for IFO analysis vis-à-vis traditionally imprinted polymer using un-functionalized GQDs (GQDs -MIP) and planer MIP-film (GQDs free) based SPCEs. Thus, this work merits special significance since no MIP for IFO is, hitherto, known to the best of our knowledge. In fact, MIP composites with m-GQDs have never been synthesized for electrochemical applications. We have used SPCE particularly for this work because of its wide acceptance toward the electrochemical sensing on account of its reproducibility and scalability (Das and Sangaranarayanan, 2015; Zhang et al., 2000). In addition, the composed ingredients such as carbon inks, polymeric binders of SPCE are quite attractive in the sense that SPCE made from using these ingredients is inexpensive and entails low background current and wide potential window for the highly sensitive electrochemical measurements (Liu et al., 2001).

Graphite powder (natural, ~325 mesh, 99.95%) was purchased from Alfa, Aesar (UK). Test analyte IFO and its interferents were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany) and Fluka (Steinheim, Germany). The initiator (α,α-Azoisobutyronitrile, AIBN), crosslinker (ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, EGDMA), 4-aminobenzoic acid, thionyl chloride, and acryl amide, were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany) and Loba Chemie (Mumbai, India). All solvents like dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), liq. NH3, and methanol, were obtained from Loba Chemie (Mumbai, India). All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as such. Phosphate buffer (pH =4.7, 0.1M) was used as the supporting electrolyte; the pH of which could be changed by the addition of few drops of either 0.1M HCl or 0.1M NaOH, as per requirement. Standard stock solution of IFO (500μgmL−1) was prepared using deionized triple distilled water (conducting range 0.06−0.07×10−6 Scm−1). All working solutions of IFO were made from the stock solution by dilution with water. The pharmaceutical formulation analyzed was Ifoxan injection (IFO, 1.0g/25mL vial, 40×103 μgmL−1) as procured from Alkem Laboratories Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Human blood plasma and urine samples were collected from the Institute of Medical Science, Banaras Hindu University (Varanasi, India), in an ice-pack and stored at −4°C. An appropriate dilution of sample is recommended to mitigate the matrix effect to a larger extent. The sample solutions were delivered with the help of highly precise micropipette obtained from Top-Tech biomedicals (Varanasi, India) [For details on various instruments used in this work, vide Supporting data Section S.1].

To synthesize N-ABA, firstly a 100mL round bottomed flask, equipped with a reflux condenser and a calcium chloride guard tube, was used in which 4-aminobenzoic acid (1.37g, 0.01mol) was reacted with thionyl chloride (3.0mL) with intermittent stirring at the room temperature for 12h, in the presence of two drops of DMF. The excess thionyl chloride was distilled off. A solid residue of 4-aminobenzoyl chloride thus obtained was washed with ethanol and dried in air. In the second step, 4-aminobenzoyl chloride (0.93g, 6mmol) and acryl amide (0.42g, 6mmol) were separately dissolved in DMF, and mixed together with continuous stirring at the room temperature for 12h to obtain the product (N-ABA). This product was characterized by FT-IR. [FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3240 (N-H stretch), 1625 (C=C ring stretch), 1640 (amide I band), 1570 (amide II band), and 1610 (C=C stretch terminal)]. This product was used as an efficient monomeric addend to GQDs-COCl through amide linkage.

For the synthesis of GQDs, first graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from graphite powder via modified Hummers method (Mondal et al., 2015). The conversion of spherical GQDs from GO flakes is reported elsewhere (Jiang et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2013; Luo et al., 2013). [For details on synthesis of GQDs, vide Supporting data Section S.2].

The functionalization of GQDs to GQDs-COCl is a necessarily step for an easier covalent bonding of N-ABA to form m-GQDs. It may be borne in the mind that GQDs (i.e. carboxylated GQDs) could not be derivatized with a basic amine (i.e. N-ABA), under moderate heating condition (≤80°C) (Lanigan and Sheppard, 2013). In this work, a mixture of as-prepared GQDs (0.1g) and thionyl chloride (20mL) was refluxed for 6h at 80°C to obtain GQDs-COCl. The excess thionyl chloride was removed with THF under vacuum distillation. To functionalize GQDs-COCl with N-ABA, a mixture of GQDs-COCl (0.05g) and N-ABA (0.20g) in 20mL anhydrous THF was refluxed at 80°C for 12h. The resulting mixture was subsequently vacuum-distilled to remove most of the excess N-ABA. The product m-GQDs was washed with ethanol, and finally dried at 100°C overnight.

For the sensor fabrication, SPCE was first cleaned by sweeping potential between −0.5 and +1.0V in 0.5MH2SO4 at 0.1Vs−1 so as to obtain a reproducible voltammogram (Vasilescu et al., 2003). Thereafter, the electrode was covered with Teflon tape leaving exclusively the working electrode area free, for the synthesis of m-GQDs -MIP at the surface.

In this work, we have preferred the “surface-grafting from” approach for the coating of m-GQDs -MIP as schematically represented in Scheme 1. In this approach, initially a prepolymer mixture of m-GQDs (5.0mg, 1.0mL DMSO) and template (IFO, 20.0mg, 400μL DMSO), in the presence of an initiator (AIBN, 5.0mg /100μL DMSO) and a crosslinker (100μL EGDMA /100μL DMSO), was made and subjected to de-aeration with N2 gas for 5min. Thereafter, the resulting mixture (20μL) was spin coated on to the surface of SPCE at 1500rpm for 15s. The firm coating of m-GQDs was due to the interaction of m-GQDs (in the prepolymer mixture) with SPCE surface through the cumulative effect of physiosorption and π- π interactions. This electrode was kept for 3h in an oven at 60°C to initiate free radical polymerization on the interface to obtain MIP-adduct@SPCE [The stoichiometry between m-GQDs and template (IFO) and conditions for polymerization were optimized as elaborated in Supporting data Section S.3 and Table S1]. After air drying, Teflon cover was removed from the modified SPCE. In order to generate imprinting cavities, template molecules were retrieved from the MIP adduct by stirring the modified electrode into the aqueous ammonia solution (25% v/v) for 40min. The complete template removal was ensured when no voltammetric response of the template was noticed. The MIP modified SPCE (m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE) so obtained was used as the working electrode for further studies. A non-imprinted polymer (NIP) modified electrode (m-GQDs -NIP@SPCE) was also prepared in the similar manner, but in the absence of template. A pristine GQDs (non-functionalized) imprinted polymer following above method was also synthesized onto the SPCE surface, termed as GQDs -MIP@SPCE, for the comparison with m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE.
Details of FT-IR, UV–Vis and Raman spectral characterizations for GQDs can be found in Supporting data Section S.5 and Fig. S2A, B and D.

FT-IR was used to elucidate the covalent grafting in anhydrous condition the amine groups of N-ABA and the acyl-functionalized GQDs (GQDs-COCl). Fig. S2C depicts stretching vibrations of GQDs-COCl [2925cm−1 (C–H stretch), 1780cm−1 (>C=O stretch), 1624cm−1 (C=C aromatic ring stretch), and 604cm−1 (C–Cl stretch)]. After the derivatization reaction between GQDs-COCl and N-ABA, the strong vibrational bands at 1640 and 1570cm−1, characteristic of amide I (>C=O stretch), and amide II (-NH bending) bands, respectively, depict amide links; while retaining other bands of N-ABA in the spectrum of m-GQDs. Notably, the C-Cl stretch (604cm−1) band disappeared after derivatization and a new N-H stretch (3320cm−1) band appeared on account of covalent amide links. Additional bands at 1420 and 1610cm−1 correspond to C-N stretch and C=C stretch (terminal double bonds), respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was also conducted to comprehend the changes in chemical composition upon derivatization ( Fig. 1A). Accordingly, the key elements of GQDs are C 1s and O 1s, which are revealed by typical peaks at ~284.3eV and ~534.1eV, respectively. In contrast, a new peak at ~401.2eV corresponding to N 1s emerged in the XPS spectrum of m-GQDs, on account of N-ABA derivation. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum of GQDs has four peaks corresponding to four carbon species: C–C/C=C at 284.3eV, C–O at 285.3eV, C=O at 287.5eV and O-C=O at 287.8eV (Fig. 1B). After derivatization with N-ABA, one additional C–N peak appeared at 285.5eV while O-C=O disappeared from the spectrum of m-GQDs (Fig. 1C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of the modified SPCEs
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrodics of bare-SPCE, GQDs, m-GQDs, GQDs -MIP and m-GQDs -MIP modified SPCEs was studied with the help of [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- redox probe in phosphate buffer (pH =5.0) ( Fig. 2A). The CV peak current gradually increased from bare to m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE; the peak separation (ΔE
p
= E
pa
- E
pc ) on the bare-SPCE, GQDs, m-GQDs, GQDs -MIP, and m-GQDs -MIP modified SPCEs are found to be 200, 180, 95, 95, and 100mV, respectively. Thus m-GQDs, GQDs -MIP and m-GQDs -MIP@SPCEs have reversibility; whereas others (bare-SPCE and GQDs-SPCE) are prone to adopt quasi-reversibility. The quasi-reversibility at bare and GQDs- SPCEs might be due to the sluggish electro oxidation of probe molecules under anodic stripping mode. On the other hand, m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE revealed a facile electron relay toward the electrode under the electrostatic pool of cationic moieties of N-ABA in acidic media. Furthermore, as compared to the simple modification with GQDs -MIP@SPCE, m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE had ~ 3-fold larger effective electrochemical surface area (A
eff ) as well as higher roughness factor (R
f ) (Table S2). To substantiate this view point, the A
eff of modified electrodes could be estimated using the following Randlese Sevick equation reported for the reversible process (Bard and Faulker, 2001):
(1)
I
p
=
(
2 . 687 × 10
5
)
n
3 / 2
ν
1 / 2
D
1 / 2
AC
0
where D (0.76×10−5 cm2 s−1) (Von Stackelberg et al., 1953) represents diffusion coefficient of ferricyanide; other parameters have their usual meanings. The roughness factor (R
f ) is equal to the ratio of effective electrochemical surface area (A
eff ) and geometrical surface area (A
geom =0.125cm2 of bare-SPCE). The high values of A
eff and R
f of m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE (Table S2) in comparison to bare, GQDs, and GQDs -MIP favored a very effective electrochemical sensing, in this work.

DPASV runs of IFO on bare-SPCE, GQDs, m-GQDs, MIP-film, GQDs -MIP, and m-GQDs -MIP modified SPCEs were also recorded (Fig. 2B). The IFO oxidation peaks at bare-SPCE, GQDs, m-GQDs-SPCE and the traditionally made MIPs (MIP-film and GQDs -MIP) are observed to be weak due to the slow electron transfer, owing to the relatively small electrochemical area. Interestingly, the DPASV response was considerably improved using m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE presumably due to a synergistic effect between the positively charged N-ABA functionalized GQDs and electron rich IFO, in terms of effective electronic transmission toward the anode under hydrogen bonding interaction. Consequently, an improved current was noticed accompanying the substantial decrease of the oxidation potential [MIP-film@SPCE (E
p =+0.18V) and m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE (E
p =+0.05V)]. This revealed that m-GQDs served as a very efficient promoter to upbring electrocatalytic action and thereby the enhanced electrode kinetics. As a matter of fact, IFO oxidation current response was increased about 3- and 7-fold at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE as compared to GQDs -MIP@SPCE and MIP-film@SPCE, respectively. This could be due to the effective charge separation at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE which increased electrocatalytic active centers and enhanced analyte diffusion for the better electrode kinetics (Kong, 2015).

As can be seen in Fig. 3A, CV anodic peak (at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE) is shifted positively with the increasing scan rate (v). Such shift could be attributed to the difficulty in the anodic stripping of accumulated analyte molecules from the electrode surface, given short duration at higher scan rates, and that required high energy. Furthermore, the oxidation peak potential is found to be shifted negatively with pH variation (2.2–10.0), in accordance with the following linear equation,
E
p
(
V
)
=
(
− 0.056 ± 0.004
)
pH +
(
0.289 ± 0.031
)
,
( R
2
= 0.98
). The slope, 0.056mV per pH, of the linear curve suggests the involvement of equal number of protons and electrons (i.e. 2H+ and 2e-). The tentative oxidation mechanism of IFO can be given as follow [Paci et al. (2001)]:
fx1

Furthermore, CV runs showed linear relationships between peak current (I
p ) and square root of the scan rate (v 1/2) as well as between peak potential (E
p ) and log v, measured on m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE:|
(2)
E
p
(
V
)
=
(
0 . 109 ± 0 . 012
)
log
ν
+
(
0 . 238 ± 0 . 015
)
,
(
R
2
= 0 . 98
)
(3)
I
p
(
μ A
)
=
(
37 . 822 ± 1 . 599
)
ν
1 / 2
+
(
− 4 . 073 ± 0 . 585
)
,
(
R
2
= 0 . 99
)

This suggested that the stripping was basically a diffusion-controlled process. Contrary to the reversible electrodics of ferricyanide at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE, the test analyte in this work showed an irreversible electron-transfer process, which can be represented as (Bard and Faulker, 2001):
(4)
[
E
p
−
E
p
/ 2
]
=
47 . 7
α
where E
p/2 is the half peak potential and α the electron transfer coefficient. The fractional value of α (0.66) supports the irreversible nature of IFO oxidation. Furthermore, chronocoulometry study revealed the standard heterogeneous rate constants (k) at GQDs -MIP@SPCE and m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE to be 6.36×10−3 and 3.16×10−2 cms−1, respectively. The higher k value at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE for IFO oxidation indicated fast electrode kinetics, without any interfacial barrier. Herein, GQDs -MIP interface (with interspersed N-ABA) did not have very fast electro oxidation revealing 10-fold inferior k value as compared to that obtained with m-GQDs -MIP interface. This suggested a pertinent role of covalently bound positively charged N-ABA moieties with GQDs at pH 4.7 to exert an electrostatically oriented electron relay from the binding sites to the electrode surface. Note that the heterogeneously dispersed N-ABA residues present during the synthesis of GQDs -MIP may not have effective electronic mediation vis-à-vis m-GQDs -MIP adhered to SPCE surface. Chronocoulometric experiments were performed on GQDs -MIP@SPCE and m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE to obtain the surface coverage (Γ°) and diffusion coefficient (D) of IFO at both types of electrodes. Accordingly, the surface coverage with 6.75×10–13 molcm−2 (3.91×1011 molecules) and 6.36×10−12 molcm−2 (10.09×1012 molecules) and diffusion coefficients 3.12×10−5 and 5.86×10−4 cm2 s−1 are obtained at GQDs -MIP@SPCE and m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE, respectively. Both the surface coverage and diffusion coefficient on m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE are found to be approximately 10-fold higher than GQDs -MIP@SPCE owing to the organized texture of m-GQDs -MIP having ability to manifest a channelized pathway for the electron transport [For details on Chronocoulometry, see Supporting data Section S.6].

Langmuir adsorption isotherm is widely used to define the mode of analyte adsorption in MIP cavities which are present in the monolayer of MIP film. Note that, the Freundlich adsorption model is suitable to account for the adsorption in the multi-layer coating. Further, Freundlich isotherm might be incompetent to define the maximum adsorption beyond the limit of saturation point. In this work, chronocoulometric data could be best fitted in the linear Langmuir adsorption isotherm on account of specific adsorption via H-bondings, showing an exclusive adsorption of one molecules of template per molecular cavity. This revealed adsorption coefficient (b) and Gibbs free energy change (ΔG =-RT ln b) as 19.78×107 Lmol−1 and – 41.63kJmol−1, respectively. The negative ΔG value indicated the spontaneity of adsorption process without any mutual interactions between analyte molecules (For details, see Supporting data Section S.7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral, morphological, surface area and conducting characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The binding mechanism between MIP (host) and IFO (guest), as suggested in Scheme 1, could be supported by FT-IR (KBr) spectroscopy (Fig. S3). The participating key functionalities of host and guest have registered downward shifts of their respective bands upon complexation via hydrogen bondings [For details, vide Supporting data Section S.8].

The morphology of the imprinted polymer was studied by the scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis. After polymerization, a compact coating of adduct polymer with m-GQDs nanostructure is developed onto the modified SPCE surface ( Fig. 4A). However, after template extraction this turned to be a porous surface (Fig. 4B). The side image of the m-GQDs -MIP modified layer on the electrode revealed the coating thickness to be 62.4nm (Fig. 4C). In the EDS spectra of m-GQDs -MIP adduct, peaks for C, N, O, P and Cl elements are observed (Fig. S4). However, with m-GQDs-MIP modified SPCE, peaks due to P and Cl elements are completely vanished owing to template removal (Fig. 4F). This also suggests the complete template extraction from the polymer-adduct motif. Furthermore, atomic force microscope (AFM, two dimensional) measurements indicated that the average size and height of m-GQDs were 7.5±0.6 and about 0.7nm, respectively (Fig. S5A). This implied that such height of dots is reportedly equal to the height of a single-layer graphene sheet (Lin et al., 2012). Fig. S5B shows that m-GQDs are actually incorporated into the imprinted polymer. The mean thickness of m-GQDs -MIP was ascertained to be 64.2nm in corroboration with that revealed by SEM (For details, vide Supporting data Section S.9).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images demonstrate that GQDs are maximally agglomerated, whereas m-GQDs are mostly homogeneously dispersed (less number of agglomeration in m-GQDs) suspension in water (Fig. 4D and E). The more dispersive property of m-GQDs than GQDs was because of N-ABA addend to GQDs which restricted its agglomeration to a major extent. The particle size distribution of GQDs and m-GQDs are 6.5±1.2nm and 7.3±0.8nm, respectively. The slight increase in the size was due to the interlocking of N-ABA to the edge of GQDs via amide-linkages. The corresponding X-ray energy dispersive spectra (EDS) support the absence of nitrogen in GQDs and the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in m-GQDs, in terms of % atomic compositions (Fig. S6A and B).

Specific surface areas for m-GQDs -MIP and m-GQDs -NIP were calculated to be 207.3 and 143.6m2 g−1, respectively. The m-GQDs -MIP showed approximately 40-folds higher electrical conductivity than the traditional GQDs -MIP (m-GQDs -MIP: 1.04×10−3 Scm−1, GQDs -MIP: 2.70×10−5 Scm−1), owing to the prevalent extended double bond conjugation in between the covalently linked N-ABA and GQDs, to facilitate uninterrupted channelized electronic transmission [For details, vide supporting data Section S.10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis in aqueous and real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3B displays DPASV response of IFO in the concentration range 0.25−121.35ngmL−1, at m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE in the aqueous medium. Accordingly, current was found to increase linearly with the increase of concentrations of the template. The peak current at m-GQDs -NIP@SPCE did not reveal perfect linearity with the concentrations (Fig. 3B, inset). Whatsoever response was observed on the NIP modified electrode due to the nonspecific adsorption of analyte could easily be washed off with water (n =3, 2.0mL). To evaluate the applicability of the proposed sensor, the concentrations of IFO in real (blood plasma, urine and pharmaceutics) samples were determined applying the standard addition method. The corresponding linear regression equations (I
p vs. C), correlation coefficients (R
2 ), recoveries, and LODs are portrayed in Table 1. A complete saturation of binding sites occurred at 121.35ngmL−1 in aqueous medium. The representative DPASV curves in real samples are shown in Fig. 3C. Evidently, DPASV peaks are found to be symmetrical for IFO in blood plasma (curves b, c and d), urine (curves f, g and h), and pharmaceutics (curves j, k and l). NIP-modified electrodes did not respond to IFO in real samples [blood plasma: curve a, urine: curve e, and pharmaceutics: curve i (Fig. 3C)]. Nevertheless, the proposed sensor should be given the similar water-washings treatment as made with NIP electrode, as a safeguard against any non-specific contributions and false-positives. It is worth comparing m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE sensor with earlier known methods employed for the determination of IFO (Table S3). Accordingly, most of the earlier methods were very low sensitive, without studying interference; whereas the present sensor is relatively high sensitive for IFO analysis in real samples, without any matrix and false-positive implications.

The cross-selectivity study with the proposed sensor, based on the specific binding interaction between the analyte and the recognition sites, is an important issue. For this, DPASV responses of IFO (24.39ngmL−1) and some interfering anticancer drugs viz; 5-fluorouracil (5-FU), cyclophosphamide (Cycl), chlorambucil (CHB), cytarabine (Cyt), and biomolecules like ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), cysteine (Cys), and uric acid (UA) were investigated using m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE and m-GQDs -NIP@SPCE. In all the cases when the interfering molecule was investigated individually, the current response was not significant. The m-GQDs -MIP@SPCE was found to be selective exclusively for IFO because of the presence of specific cavities generated under the phenomenal imprinting effect, in the polymer matrix. Both MIP and NIP modified electrodes revealed some DPASV responses for all the interferents, when studied individually, without undergoing washing treatment (Fig. S7). We have also examined the parallel cross-reactivity with a binary mixtures of template and interferent(s) (taken in 1: 200 concentration ratio) (Fig. S7). To further confirm the selectivity of proposed sensor, imprinting factor (β= i
MIP
/i
NIP ) was calculated. Imprinting factor is a measure of the strength of interaction of the imprinted polymer towards the template molecule, calculated by the ratio of the current obtained on m-GQDs -MIP and m-GQDs -NIP modified SPCEs. The calculated β for IFO, 5-FU, Cycl, CHB, Cyt, AA, DA, Cys, and UA are 15.4, 1.8, 1.3, 1.1, 0.7 1.6, 1.4, 0.66, and 1.7, respectively. It was found that proposed sensor has β (15.4) for IFO, which is about more than 9 times higher than the interfering compounds.

The propose sensor showed high precision in terms of its fabrication and regeneration with high endurance (three weeks duration) and the thermal stability of coating material (~ 291°C) (For details, vide Supporting data Section S.11, Fig. S8 and S9).

For the first time, the IFO selective m-GQDs -MIP based nanocomposite was synthesized directly at the surface of SPCE. Herein, the covalently bound GQDs with monomeric units of N-ABA were found to be advantageous to obtain a sensory platform with larger surface area for the faster ingress and egress of analyte. The detection limit was realized to be as low as 0.177ngmL−1 and the imprinting factor was found to be as high as 15.43. The ultra sensitivity could be attributed to higher electrode kinetics under electrocatalytic effect and good electrical conductivity of m-GQDs. The superb selectivity was derived from the higher imprinting factor of imprinted sites in the m-GQDs -MIP composite. The electrochemical detection of IFO was feasible in biological and pharmaceutical samples, without any cross-reactivity, non-specific (false-positive), and matrix complications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (AK) acknowledges the financial support as Senior Research Fellowship (No. 09/013(0513)/2013-EMR-I) from Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), New Delhi, India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.02.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials, Polymers and Devices: Materials Functionalization and Device
